itemListElement.name: Land Rover Freelander Series 1 (L314) 1.8 K-Series / 2.3 K-Series

mainEntity.name: How much does a K-Series 1.8/2.3 engine replacement cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned K-Series 1.8/2.3 typically costs £900–£1,800 supply only, with used units from £500–£900. Fitting adds £600–£1,000 depending on labour rates and vehicle access. Specialist K-Series rebuilds may cost more.

mainEntity.name: Is the K-Series 1.8/2.3 a reliable engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: The K-Series has a mixed reputation (2.5★). While the engine is lightweight and refined, its well-documented head gasket failure — exacerbated by the use of a low-torque stretch bolt design and inadequate cooling system — gives it a poor reliability record.

mainEntity.name: What is the most common K-Series failure?

acceptedAnswer.text: Head gasket failure is the most common and notorious issue — caused by the original elastomer gasket design and the use of stretch bolts that lose clamping force over time. Also common: coolant system leaks, thermostat housing failures, and liner movement.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover models use the K-Series?

acceptedAnswer.text: The K-Series powered the Freelander 1 (L314) in 1.8L (18K4F) and later 2.3L (23K4F) forms from 1997 to 2006.

mainEntity.name: How long does the K-Series 1.8/2.3 last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With the original elastomer head gasket and stretch bolts, many K-Series engines failed before 80,000 miles. However, correctly rebuilt engines using the later metal MLS head gasket and upgraded bolts often exceed 150,000–200,000 miles when properly maintained.

items.question: How much does a K-Series 1.8/2.3 engine replacement cost?
items.answer: A reconditioned K-Series 1.8/2.3 typically costs £900–£1,800 supply only, with used units from £500–£900. Fitting adds £600–£1,000 depending on labour rates and vehicle access. Specialist K-Series rebuilds may cost more.

items.question: Is the K-Series 1.8/2.3 a reliable engine?
items.answer: The K-Series has a mixed reputation (2.5★). While the engine is lightweight and refined, its well-documented head gasket failure — exacerbated by the use of a low-torque stretch bolt design and inadequate cooling system — gives it a poor reliability record. However, correctly rebuilt examples with upgraded MLS gaskets and proper coolant are much more durable.

items.question: What is the most common K-Series failure?
items.answer: Head gasket failure is the most common and notorious issue — caused by the original elastomer gasket design and the use of stretch bolts that lose clamping force over time. Also common: coolant system leaks, thermostat housing failures, and liner movement in high-mileage engines.

items.question: Which Land Rover models use the K-Series?
items.answer: The K-Series powered the Freelander 1 (L314) in 1.8L (18K4F) and later 2.3L (23K4F) forms from 1997 to 2006.

items.question: How long does the K-Series 1.8/2.3 last?
items.answer: With the original elastomer head gasket and stretch bolts, many K-Series engines failed before 80,000 miles. However, correctly rebuilt engines using the later metal MLS (Multi-Layer Steel) head gasket and upgraded bolts often exceed 150,000–200,000 miles when properly maintained with the correct coolant.
